metric_value: Longhorn represents an incremental step past XP; Blackcomb is positioned as the longer-term architectural next generation. Transition spans multiple Windows releases.
notes: Program agenda segment "Peter Kastner: Differences Among Windows XP, Longhorn and Blackcomb"

metric_value: Provides Kastner's guidance on how enterprise buyers should approach Windows upgrades for 2002 and beyond given Longhorn/Blackcomb horizon.
notes: Segment "Peter Kastner: Windows Upgrade Strategy for 2002 and Beyond"

metric_value: Microsoft Tablet PC initiative discussed as a strategic platform bet by Microsoft to extend Windows into pen-centric form factors.
notes: Segment "Isaac Ro: Microsoft's Tablet PC Initiative"

metric_value: Program compares Microsoft's Pocket PC to Palm Pilot; Microsoft pursuing a two-pronged approach and targeted markets distinct from Palm's.
notes: Segments "The Pocket PC vs. the Palm Pilot" and "Markets Served by Microsoft's Pocket PC"

metric_value: Implications of Microsoft's MiPad and 'Dr. Who' speech research initiatives; current technology limitations discussed.
notes: Segments "Implications of Microsoft's MiPad and Dr. Who Initiatives" and "Limitations of Current Speech Recognition Technology"

metric_value: Kastner covers where Microsoft is heading in knowledge/document management — integration into OS and apps rather than standalone KM products.
Kastner explains how unified storage (the WinFS relational-storage vision) fits into Microsoft's Blackcomb plans.
notes: Segment "Peter Kastner: How Unified Storage Fits Into Microsoft's Vision for Blackcomb"

metric_value: Kastner introduces Aberdeen's ACTR (Adopt / Contain / Train / Retire) technology-decision model for enterprise planning.
notes: Segment "Peter Kastner: Aberdeen Group's ACTR Model"

metric_value: Microsoft product manager Shaw describes how .NET shifts Microsoft's business model toward services and platform monetization.
notes: Segment "Jackson Shaw: The Effect of .NET on the Microsoft Business Model"

metric_value: Longhorn shipped January 2007 as Windows Vista; delayed from original 2004/2005 target and with reduced feature set (WinFS/unified storage cut).
Blackcomb as a distinct release never shipped; features were split between Vista (2007) and Windows 7 (2009).
MiPad remained a Microsoft Research prototype; never shipped commercially. Speech technology ambitions were partly realized later via Cortana, Azure Speech.
